articles.headline: Why are there hydration breaks at the 2026 World Cup, and why has FIFA been criticised?
articles.description: All 104 matches at the 2026 World Cup are stopping for two hydration breaks. Why have they been stipulated by FIFA, and why have they been criticised?

images.name:
Players of Tunisia take drinks at the hydration break during the FIFA World Cup 2026 Group F match between Sweden and Tunisia at Monterrey Stadium
 [1296x729]
images.alt: Players of Tunisia take drinks at the hydration break during the FIFA World Cup 2026 Group F match between Sweden and Tunisia at Monterrey Stadium

images.name: Enzo Fernandez #8 of Chelsea FC drinks water in a hydration break during the FIFA Club World Cup 2025 semi-final match between Fluminense FC and Chelsea FC at MetLife Stadium [1296x729]
images.caption: Argentina's Enzo Fernández said he felt "dizzy" while playing in "very dangerous" temperatures during last summer's Club World Cup in the United States.
images.alt: Enzo Fernandez #8 of Chelsea FC drinks water in a hydration break during the FIFA Club World Cup 2025 semi-final match between Fluminense FC and Chelsea FC at MetLife Stadium

images.name:
Electronic screen displaying hydration break information during the FIFA World Cup 2026 Group C match between Haiti and Scotland at Boston Stadium
 [1296x729]
images.caption: Advertising is prominent on stadium screens during drinks breaks at the World Cup.
images.alt: Electronic screen displaying hydration break information during the FIFA World Cup 2026 Group C match between Haiti and Scotland at Boston Stadium

images.name:
Virgil van Dijk (Netherlands) looks on during Group F FIFA World Cup 2026, Netherlands and Japan, Dallas Stadium
 [1296x729]
images.caption: Virgil van Dijk has been critical of the hydration breaks introduced at this summer's World Cup.
images.alt: Virgil van Dijk (Netherlands) looks on during Group F FIFA World Cup 2026, Netherlands and Japan, Dallas Stadium

images.name:
The Brazil team talk during a hydration break during the FIFA World Cup 2026 Group C match between Brazil and Morocco at New York New Jersey Stadium
 [1296x729]
images.caption: Carlo Ancelotti was thankful for the opportunity to speak to his players when Brazil found themselves a goal behind to Morocco in the first half of their World Cup opener.
images.alt: The Brazil team talk during a hydration break during the FIFA World Cup 2026 Group C match between Brazil and Morocco at New York New Jersey Stadium

images.name:
Myung-Bo Hong, Head Coach of Korea Republic, gives his players instructions during a hydration break during the FIFA World Cup 2026 Group A match between Korea Republic and Czechia
 [1296x729]
images.caption: Hydration breaks provide a window for coaches to gather their players and tweak their tactics.
images.alt: Myung-Bo Hong, Head Coach of Korea Republic, gives his players instructions during a hydration break during the FIFA World Cup 2026 Group A match between Korea Republic and Czechia

images.name:
Germany's forward #26 Deniz Undav, Germany's midfielder #13 Pascal Gross, Germany's midfielder #17 Florian Wirtz, Germany's midfielder #05 Aleksandar Pavlovic and teammates celebrate
 [1296x729]
images.caption: Drinks breaks could have a major effect on the outcome of matches at the World Cup.
images.alt: Germany's forward #26 Deniz Undav, Germany's midfielder #13 Pascal Gross, Germany's midfielder #17 Florian Wirtz, Germany's midfielder #05 Aleksandar Pavlovic and teammates celebrate
images.credit: Kirill KUDRYAVTSEV / AFP via Getty Images

article.headline: Cameroon beat group-winners Brazil but still exit World Cup
article.description: Cameroon defeated a second-string Brazil team 1-0 in their final Group G match but it was not enough to advance to the World Cup knockout stages.
article.linkText: Cameroon beat group-winners Brazil but exit WC

article.story:
A late goal by skipper Vincent Aboubakar ended Cameroon's 20-year wait for a win at the World Cup as the Indomitable Lions bowed out in style by beating a much-changed Brazil 1-0 at the Lusail Stadium on Friday.

With qualification already in the bag after wins over Serbia and Switzerland, Brazil still won Group G, ahead of Switzerland on goal difference, and will face Group H runners-up South Korea, who beat Portugal 2-1, in the next round.

- Stream on ESPN+: LaLiga, Bundesliga, more (U.S.)

Cameroon, whose last win at the World Cup was in 2002 against Saudi Arabia, were on the back foot for most of the match but sprung to life at the death. Aboubakar powered a header past Ederson then was sent off for collecting a second yellow for taking off his jersey in celebration.

The win was Cameroon's first against Brazil at the World Cup and the South American giants' first defeat by African opposition at the finals.

Brazil had by far the larger share of possession and carved out many more chances than their opponents, but in the end it was Cameroon who got the only statistic that actually counts.

"We dominated the match and had several chances, the only thing missing was a goal," Brazil's Eder Militao said.

"We should have scored but a lack of attention in one play allowed them to score. It's frustrating considering the way we played."

The match played out in a relatively subdued atmosphere in the cavernous Lusail Stadium, the tournament's biggest venue, with Brazil's reserves eager to impress but Tite's side unable to assert their usual flair.

- World Cup knockout bracket, results and fixtures

Gabriel Martinelli was Brazil's liveliest player and nearly put the five-time champions ahead in the 14th minute when he was picked out by Fred's ball over the top, but the Arsenal forward's header drew a fine reaction save from Devis Epassy.

Cameroon offered nearly no attacking threat until the first half injury time, when Nicolas Moumi Ngamaleu's cross was met with a powerful downward header by Bryan Mbeumo that Ederson clawed away one-handed at full stretch.

Tite had seen enough and made a triple change soon after the interval, bringing on Marquinho for the injured Alex Telles, and replacing Rodrygo and Fred with Everton Ribeiro and Bruno Guimaraes.

The substitutes stirred Brazil into a sudden flurry of chances with Epassy forced into action three times in as many minutes.

First, Martinelli stung the Cameroon keeper's palms with a snap shot in the 56th minute.

Next, Eder Militao tried his luck from the ensuing corner coaxing a fumble out of the Cameroon keeper, who recovered to scramble the ball out to safety, before the lively Antony got in on the act, forcing Epassy into a diving save to keep out his curling effort.

Tite sent on Pedro in the 64th minute and Rafinha in the 79th as Brazil sought to save their perfect record in the group, but Cameroon clung on until Aboubakar arrived in the right place at the right time to seize his moment.
